NOFO.definition: The official posting that announces a grant opportunity.
NOFO.plain_english: This is the grant listing itself — the document that says what the agency wants, who can apply, how to apply, and when it is due.
NOFO.why_it_matters: If you miss what the NOFO says, you can waste time on a grant you cannot win.

Assistance Listing.definition: The federal listing number/category for a grant program.
Assistance Listing.plain_english: The old CFDA name still gets used sometimes, but the current term is Assistance Listing.
Assistance Listing.why_it_matters: It helps identify the correct federal program and is often referenced in award paperwork.

Eligibility.definition: The rules that determine who may apply.
Eligibility.plain_english: This tells you whether your organization type, location, or project can apply.
Eligibility.why_it_matters: If you don’t meet eligibility, the application is dead on arrival.

Match / Cost Share.definition: Money or in-kind support that the applicant contributes.
Match / Cost Share.plain_english: The grant only pays part of the bill; you cover the rest with your own money, partner support, or approved in-kind value.
Match / Cost Share.why_it_matters: Some grants require it, and failing to document it can disqualify the application.

Indirect Costs.definition: Shared organizational expenses that support the project but are not direct project costs.
Indirect Costs.plain_english: Think rent, admin, accounting, and the lights staying on.
Indirect Costs.why_it_matters: You need to know whether the grant allows indirect costs and at what rate.

Supplanting.definition: Using grant money to replace money that should already be covering the expense.
Supplanting.plain_english: The grant cannot just take the place of money you were already supposed to spend.
Supplanting.why_it_matters: A lot of grants ban it outright.

Period of Performance.definition: The time window in which the grant-funded work must happen.
Period of Performance.plain_english: Start to finish, this is the window where the project has to be active and expenses have to fit.
Period of Performance.why_it_matters: Costs outside the period usually don’t count.

Match Rate.definition: The percentage of the project cost you must provide.
Match Rate.plain_english: If the grant says 20% match, you bring 20 cents for every dollar the grant covers.
Match Rate.why_it_matters: This affects budget size and whether the grant is realistic for your organization.

SAM.gov.definition: The federal registration system used by organizations that want to do business with the government.
SAM.gov.plain_english: You usually need this before you can get paid by a federal grant.
SAM.gov.why_it_matters: Expired registration can kill an award or block payment.

UEI.definition: The identifier used by the federal government to identify your organization.
UEI.plain_english: It’s the number that says, 'this is your organization' in the federal system.
UEI.why_it_matters: It’s required for most federal grant workflows.

MTDC.definition: A federal budgeting base used to calculate indirect costs.
MTDC.plain_english: Not every dollar in the budget counts toward the indirect-cost math.
MTDC.why_it_matters: Using the wrong base can wreck your budget math.

Formula Grant.definition: A grant distributed based on a formula, not a competitive score.
Formula Grant.plain_english: The money is often allocated based on rules like population or need.
Formula Grant.why_it_matters: These are easier to predict than competitive grants.

Competitive Grant.definition: A grant awarded through a review and scoring process.
Competitive Grant.plain_english: You’re competing against other applicants, and the best proposal wins.
Competitive Grant.why_it_matters: You need a stronger narrative and compliance package.
